train_pairs.target: I came and saw the man and the dog.
You(sg) sing and I get up.
He saw the house and I took the pig.
She will get up and will take the bread.
We will go away and will sing.
They eat the bread and drink the water.

train_pairs.source: Anon ye weng wengambaranuuwa awon ye weng wengambaraneen.
train_pairs.target: We will hear the dog and he will hear the pig.
I drank the water and she saw the fish.
He takes the bread and eats it.

test_pairs.source: We will go away and they will come.
I take the pig and he takes the fish.
He saw the water and the house and heard the dog.
test_pairs.target: Oka ambiwa wedmene anon ye weng wengamboroon.

test_pairs.source: You(sg) eat the bread and sing.

verification.sentence_tested: We will go away and they will come.
verification.hypothesis:
I'll attempt to translate the sentence into Yonggom, a language spoken in Papua New Guinea. However, please note that I may not be entirely accurate due to the limited availability of Yonggom language resources.

The sentence "We will go away and they will come" can be broken down into its components:

- "We will go away" can be translated to "Mere wala" (Mere means "we" and wala means "go away" or "leave").
- "and" can be translated to "a" (a conjunction used to connect two clauses).
- "they will come" can be translated to "Meru wala" (Meru means "they" and wala means "come").

Combining these components, a possible translation of the sentence into Yonggom could be:

"Mere wala a Meru wala"

Please note that this is just an attempt and may not be entirely accurate.
